agent_output:
{
  "summary": {
    "verdict": "BLOCK",
    "one_liner": "Financial calculation has multiple critical flaws: negative payouts can send money backwards, floating-point arithmetic will silently corrupt cent values, and zero-delivery edge case charges commission on nothing."
  },

  "critical_findings": [
    {
      "title": "Negative payout triggers reverse bank transfer",
      "risk": "If deductions exceed basePay + bonusPay (e.g., damage charges, order cancellations), totalEarnings goes negative. A 15% commission on a negative number reduces the magnitude, and the function returns a negative value. This negative value is passed directly to a bank transfer API — depending on the provider, this could initiate a debit/charge from the courier's account or throw an unhandled error.",
      "severity": "Critical",
      "location": "calculateWeeklyPayout — no floor/clamp on totalEarnings or payout",
      "reproduction": "Courier with 1 delivery: basePay=5, bonusPay=0, deductions=50. totalEarnings=-45, commission=-6.75, payout=-38.25. Bank transfer initiated for -$38.25.",
      "fix": "Add `const payout = Math.max(0, totalEarnings - commission);` at minimum. Better: separate the negative-balance case into a distinct billing/collections flow rather than silently clamping or reversing.",
      "time_to_fix": "30 minutes",
      "acceptance_criteria_affected": "Couriers must never be debited via the payout path; negative balances require separate handling."
    },
    {
      "title": "Floating-point arithmetic corrupts monetary values",
      "risk": "IEEE 754 floating-point cannot represent many decimal values exactly. Repeated addition/subtraction across potentially hundreds of deliveries accumulates rounding errors. For a function that directly initiates bank transfers, even a 1-cent discrepancy per payout across thousands of couriers creates accounting reconciliation nightmares and potential legal/compliance violations.",
      "severity": "Critical",
      "location": "calculateWeeklyPayout — all arithmetic uses native floating-point (number type)",
      "reproduction": "Three deliveries with basePay values of 0.1 + 0.2 + 0.3 = 0.6000000000000001 in JS. After commission: 0.5100000000000001. Over 10,000 couriers/week, cumulative drift becomes material.",
      "fix": "Perform all arithmetic in integer cents (multiply by 100 at ingestion, divide by 100 only at final output) or use a decimal library like decimal.js. Round the final payout with `Math.round(payout * 100) / 100` as an immediate stopgap.",
      "time_to_fix": "1-2 hours for cents-based refactor; 5 minutes for rounding stopgap",
      "acceptance_criteria_affected": "Payout amount must be exact to the cent for bank transfer and tax reporting."
    },
    {
      "title": "Commission applied to gross earnings including deductions creates incorrect payout",
      "risk": "The commission is calculated on totalEarnings, which already has deductions subtracted. This means the platform takes 15% of (gross - deductions) rather than 15% of gross. If deductions are courier-borne costs (damages, equipment), the platform is subsidizing those by reducing its own commission proportionally. If this is unintentional, it's a revenue leak; if intentional, the variable naming obscures the business logic. Either way, auditors and couriers will compute different numbers.",
      "severity": "High",
      "location": "calculateWeeklyPayout — `const commission = totalEarnings * 0.15` where totalEarnings already includes deductions",
      "reproduction": "Courier: basePay=100, bonusPay=10, deductions=20. Current: commission = (90)*0.15 = 13.50, payout = 76.50. If commission should be on gross: commission = (110)*0.15 = 16.50, payout = 73.50. Difference of $3.00 per delivery.",
      "fix": "Clarify business intent. If commission is on gross: compute `grossEarnings` (base+bonus) separately, apply commission to that, then subtract deductions. Rename variables to make the order of operations explicit and auditable.",
      "time_to_fix": "30 minutes (code) + business decision required",
      "acceptance_criteria_affected": "Commission calculation must match the contractual terms with couriers."
    }
  ],

  "important_issues": [
    {
      "title": "No empty-deliveries guard",
      "description": "If a courier has zero deliveries, the function returns 0 and presumably triggers a $0.00 bank transfer. Most payment processors reject or flag zero-amount transfers, potentially causing batch failures or alerts.",
      "location": "calculateWeeklyPayout — no early return for empty deliveries array"
    },
    {
      "title": "No validation of delivery field types",
      "description": "If any delivery record has null/undefined for basePay, bonusPay, or deductions (e.g., partial database migration, schema drift), the arithmetic produces NaN, which propagates silently to the bank transfer amount.",
      "location": "calculateWeeklyPayout — inner loop accesses fields without null checks"
    },
    {
      "title": "No idempotency or duplicate protection",
      "description": "If this function is called twice for the same courier in the same week (retry, cron overlap, queue double-delivery), two bank transfers are initiated. There is no payout-ID, deduplication key, or status check.",
      "location": "calculateWeeklyPayout — stateless, no dedup mechanism"
    }
  ],

  "suggestions": [
    "Return a PayoutResult object (amount, breakdown, courierId, periodStart, periodEnd) instead of a bare number — this gives downstream consumers an audit trail and makes logging/debugging trivial.",
    "Add a sanity-check upper bound (e.g., flag payouts > $10,000 for manual review) to catch data corruption before it reaches the bank.",
    "Wrap the entire payout path in a database transaction with a unique constraint on (courierId, weekOf) to prevent double-pays."
  ],

  "production_readiness": {
    "deployment_risks": "Function has no guards against negative, NaN, or astronomically large values — any of these will produce a real bank transfer with bad data.",
    "rollback_capability": "Bank transfers are not rollback-safe. Once initiated, reversals require manual intervention and take days. Code rollback does not undo sent payments.",
    "backward_compatibility": "N/A — standalone function, no API consumers to break.",
    "monitoring_coverage": "No logging, no metrics, no alerting. A bad payout would only be discovered when a courier or accountant complains.",
    "breaking_changes": {
      "api_breaking": false,
      "db_destructive": false,
      "shared_library": false,
      "auth_security": false,
      "data_pipeline": true,
      "config_changes": false,
      "details": ["This function feeds directly into a payment pipeline — any arithmetic error becomes a real financial transaction."]
    },
    "safe_to_deploy": false,
    "rollback_plan": "Cannot safely roll back a bad payment batch after bank transfers are initiated. Must fix before deploying."
  },

  "escalations": [],

  "confidence": {
    "level": 95,
    "high_confidence_areas": ["Negative payout path is trivially reproducible", "Floating-point drift is a known JS behavior", "Commission-on-net vs commission-on-gross is ambiguous in the code"],
    "low_confidence_areas": ["Exact business intent for commission calculation order — need product/legal clarification"],
    "assumptions": ["delivery.basePay, bonusPay, deductions are intended to be numeric dollar amounts", "The return value is used as-is for the bank transfer amount with no downstream validation"]
  }
}
